bigPromise: Learn what makes confidence drop, then pick one small reset move.

artifact.name: Reset Ring
artifact.concept: A gold ring of six small lights — one lights per lesson. It is the hero of every screen and shows your confidence reset filling up, one tiny win at a time.
artifact.visual: A 248px ring sitting at the top of the screen as the HERO. A faint gold track circle (stroke var(--gold-bg)) holds six evenly spaced 30px nodes around it. Empty nodes are pale: white fill, thin gold-line border, soft sh-1 shadow. The current lesson's node 'breathes' (pulsing gold halo). A lit node fills with the gold gradient (var(--gold-soft) to var(--gold)) and carries the small white icon of the reset move you chose for that lesson — so the ring is not six identical dots but six distinct gold tokens: a trigger eye, a speech reply, a proof check, a fit compass, a daily loop, a brave step. The ring center is a small gold star emblem above a Cormorant count 'N/6' and the uppercase label RESET RING. A one-line italic caption under the ring narrates progress ('Two lights on. Four more to a full reset ring.'). Empty state: one breathing node, count 0/6, soft and calm. Full state: all six gold tokens glow, the center count reads 6/6, a final gentle ring-wide shimmer.
artifact.fillUnit: One node lights gold and receives the icon of the reset move chosen in that lesson.
artifact.saveMoment: On Save: the chosen choice's icon detaches as a floating gold token (.fly, gold-gradient, sh-gold) and travels on a .6s spring (cubic-bezier(.34,1.56,.64,1)) from the tapped card into the next empty ring node. As it lands, that node runs the 'drop' keyframe (scale .3 to 1.22 to 1), switches to .lit gold, and the hero halo fires a one-shot 'glow' pulse. The center count ticks up with a quick number bump, the caption rewrites, and the Estrella line turns soft-green 'win'. On lesson 6 the same flight lands the last token, then the whole ring runs a brief sweep shimmer and the center star pulses once — the reset is complete.
artifact.renderApproach: Reuse confidence-simple-v2.html verbatim as the shell — same tokens (--bg #FCFAF4, --card, --cream, golds #C49A3F/#E6CD86/#A07A2C, --gold-bg, shadows sh-1/2/3/sh-gold, --spring). Keep the existing .hero/.ring/.node/.ring-core/.ring-emblem markup and the SVG track circle. Drive it from data: an array of 6 lesson objects each holding {idea, say, situationIcon, situationText, ask, choices:[{key,label,icon}], helpful, feedback[byKey], estrella, fills, tokenIcon}. Node positions are the six coordinates already in the file (top, upper-right, lower-right, bottom, lower-left, upper-left). State = current lesson index n (0..5) and a savedTokens array. On load, nodes 0..n-1 render .lit with their saved tokenIcon innerHTML; node n gets .active (breathing); the rest stay empty. Render the lesson card below (.step shows 'Lesson n of 6 · See it', .idea in Cormorant, .say, .situation pill, .ask, .choices). Selecting a choice adds .sel and enables the CTA; Save runs the existing fly-token routine pointing at node n, calls lightRing(node n, chosenIcon), increments the count, advances n, and swaps the card to lesson n+1 (no page reload — just re-render the card region and re-point .active). Respect prefers-reduced-motion: skip the flight, light the node directly. Each lesson supplies its own tokenIcon so the ring reads as six distinct gold moves, not a ring of identical dots.
artifact.accent: gold (#C49A3F / #E6CD86 / #A07A2C) with a single calm success tint var(--ok) #4C9C7A used only for the Estrella 'win' chip

lessons.sentence: Confidence is not who you are. It is a state that changes with the moment, the room, and the people in it.
lessons.situation: You go quiet the second someone senior joins the meeting.
choices: I felt judged
The moment felt big
I compared myself
lessons.feedback: Good. You just named the real trigger instead of blaming yourself.
lessons.helpful: all valid
lessons.estrella: Pick the one that feels most true right now.
lessons.fills: First node lights gold with a small eye token — the trigger you named.

lessons.sentence: The voice that says you are not good enough is trying to protect you, but it is not telling the truth. You can answer it back.
lessons.situation: A voice says: they will see you are not ready for this.
choices: Push it away
Believe it
Answer it with a fair line
lessons.feedback: Yes. You don't delete the voice, you answer it with something fair and true.
lessons.helpful: Answer it with a fair line
lessons.estrella: Would you say that thought to a friend? Be that fair.
lessons.fills: Second node lights with a small reply token — the answer you gave the critic.

lessons.sentence: Your brain holds onto failures and forgets wins. Write the real ones down so doubt can't quietly erase them.
lessons.situation: Doubt says: you have never really done anything big.
choices: I am a hard worker
I led the move that cut costs 12%
People probably like me
lessons.feedback: That one is real and specific. Facts like this beat any feeling of doubt.
lessons.helpful: I led the move that cut costs 12%
lessons.estrella: Only real, specific facts count. One proof beats ten worries.
lessons.fills: Third node lights with a small check token — a proven win added to your file.

lessons.sentence: A rejection is information about one fit at one time. It is not a final score on your worth.
lessons.situation: You were turned down for the senior role you wanted.
choices: I am not good enough
They wanted one skill I can build
I should stop trying
lessons.feedback: Right. That makes the no small and fixable instead of large and personal.
lessons.helpful: They wanted one skill I can build
lessons.estrella: Let a no sharpen you, never shrink you.
lessons.fills: Fourth node lights with a small compass token — the no, read as a fit, not a verdict.

lessons.sentence: Confidence built on mood fails on hard days. A small daily habit keeps you steady whether you feel like it or not.
lessons.situation: It's a tired, stressful morning and you don't feel brave.
choices: Wait until I feel ready
Do my small daily habit anyway
Skip today
lessons.feedback: Yes. The habit runs even when the mood doesn't. That is the whole point.
lessons.helpful: Do my small daily habit anyway
lessons.estrella: Confidence is repetition, not motivation. Keep the small habit.
lessons.fills: Fifth node lights with a small loop token — the daily habit you chose to keep.

lessons.sentence: You don't think your way to confidence. You do one slightly hard thing, survive it, and your belief updates.
lessons.situation: You've been avoiding asking your boss for a bigger budget.
choices: Keep avoiding it
Ask, even if the answer is maybe
Wait for the perfect moment
lessons.feedback: That's earned confidence. The win is asking and surviving, not the answer.
lessons.helpful: Ask, even if the answer is maybe
lessons.estrella: One small brave step teaches your brain more than a week of reading.
lessons.fills: Sixth node lights with a small step token — and the whole ring shimmers full: reset complete.

bigPromise: Help people know exactly what to remember you for.

artifact.name: The Profile Glow
artifact.concept: A vertical reputation meter that fills with light as your name becomes Clear, Proof-backed, and Useful — 12 lessons turn a faint profile into one that glows.
artifact.visual: A tall rounded-capsule meter (the HERO, centered, ~96px wide x 360px tall) standing upright on warm white, like a thermometer of reputation. Inside it: 12 stacked segment-cells climbing bottom to top. Empty cells are faint cream (--gold-bg) with a hairline gold border; filled cells glow with the gold gradient (--gold-soft to --gold). The 12 cells group into three labeled bands of 4: a CLEAR band (bottom), a PROOF band (middle), a USEFUL band (top) — tiny uppercase --mute labels sit to the right of each band. At the very top of the capsule sits a small round 'name disc' — a gold-rimmed circle with a person glyph that represents YOU; when empty it is grey and dim, and it brightens stage by stage as bands complete. A soft radial gold halo (the 'glow') sits behind the whole capsule and grows in opacity as more cells light. Center-bottom shows a Cormorant count: e.g. 7/12, with the active band name beneath it ('Building proof'). Empty state = a dim grey disc on an unlit cream tube, caption 'A faint name. Light it up.' Full state = all 12 cells lit, the name disc fully gold and beaming, halo at full strength, caption 'People know exactly what you are for.'
artifact.fillUnit: One saved lesson lights exactly one segment-cell (the next one up), tinted by that lesson's quality: clear-gold, proof-gold-deep, or useful-warm. Completing all 4 cells in a band snaps that band's side-label to lit gold and brightens the name disc one stage.
artifact.saveMoment: On Save, the chosen answer's small icon detaches and flies (cubic-bezier(.34,1.56,.64,1), ~640ms) from the choice card up into the next empty cell. On arrival the cell does a spring 'pour': it scale-pops from .3 to 1 while the gold gradient floods upward like liquid light filling the cell (a clip-path/height transition from 0 to full). The capsule's halo pulses brighter once (radial-gradient opacity .16 to .30 and back). If the save completes a band, the side-label ignites with a brief gold shimmer sweep and the name disc brightens with a soft bloom. Reduced-motion: cell fills instantly, halo steps up, no flight.
artifact.renderApproach: Single capsule built as a flex column of 12 .cell divs inside a rounded container (border-radius:48px; background:var(--cream); border:1.5px solid var(--gold-line); box-shadow:var(--sh-2) plus inset top highlight). Each .cell is flex:1 with a 2px gap; default background:var(--gold-bg); .cell.lit gets background:linear-gradient(180deg,var(--gold-soft),var(--gold)) and box-shadow:var(--sh-gold), revealed via a child .fill div whose height animates 0->100% (transition height .5s var(--spring)). Quality tint = data-q attribute swapping the gradient stops (clear=--gold-soft/--gold, proof=--gold/--gold-deep, useful=--gold-soft/#D8A94A). Three band labels are absolutely positioned to the right at 4-cell intervals; .band.done flips color from --mute to --gold-deep with a shimmer keyframe. Name disc = a 46px circle absolutely placed atop the capsule, background grey radial when dim, transitioning to radial-gradient(circle at 36% 30%,var(--gold-soft),var(--gold-deep)) + box-shadow:var(--sh-gold) as stages complete. Halo = a ::before radial-gradient(circle, rgba(196,154,63,VAR) 0%, transparent 62%) behind the capsule, --VAR rising with progress. Fly token reuses the V2 .fly pattern (fixed 40px gold rounded square, innerHTML cloned from chosen .c-ic, getBoundingClientRect from card to target cell, 600ms spring). Count + band name use Cormorant Garamond (--ink with --gold-deep accent); all tokens reuse the V2 :root palette, shadows, and --spring easing exactly. Fully buildable with the confidence-simple-v2.html stylesheet as the base — only the hero markup changes from a ring to this capsule.
artifact.accent: gold (#C49A3F / #E6CD86 / #A07A2C) with a single warm amber tint (#D8A94A) reserved for the USEFUL band, kept inside the Estrella palette

lessons.idea: Your brand is what they say later.
lessons.sentence: Your brand is what people say about you when you are not in the room. It is your reputation, made on purpose.
lessons.situation: Two coworkers talk about you after a meeting. You are not there.
choices: "She posts a lot online."
"She always spots the risk early."
"She has a nice profile photo."
lessons.feedback: Yes. People remember the value you bring, not your noise.
lessons.helpful: "She always spots the risk early."
lessons.estrella: Pick the line that names your value, not your noise.
lessons.fills: Lights the first CLEAR cell (clear-gold) and the name disc flickers on.

lessons.idea: Say what you do in one line.
lessons.sentence: One clear sentence beats a job title: who you help, what you fix, and the real result they get.
lessons.situation: Someone asks, "So, what do you do?" at an event.
choices: "I am an HR manager."
"I help retailers keep new hires so they save training money."
"I do a bit of everything in people stuff."
lessons.feedback: That is it. A title is a box. This shows why you matter.
lessons.helpful: "I help retailers keep new hires so they save training money."
lessons.estrella: Name who you help and the result they actually feel.
lessons.fills: Lights the second CLEAR cell (clear-gold).

lessons.sentence: Before you build a brand, find out how people see you now. Ask honest colleagues, not just kind friends.
lessons.situation: You want to know your real reputation at work today.
choices: Ask close friends who always praise you.
Ask a few honest colleagues what they say about your work.
Guess, because you know yourself best.
lessons.feedback: Right. Honest colleagues hand you real evidence to work with.
lessons.helpful: Ask a few honest colleagues what they say about your work.
lessons.estrella: Honest feedback shows the gap you cannot see alone.
lessons.fills: Lights the third CLEAR cell (clear-gold).

lessons.idea: Connect your jobs into one story.
lessons.sentence: A story links your past, your turning point, and where you head now, so your path looks chosen, not random.
lessons.situation: Your career looks like three unrelated jobs.
choices: List every job and let them figure it out.
Find the one thread that runs through all three.
Say your career has no real direction yet.
lessons.feedback: Yes. One repeating thread turns a messy path into a journey.
lessons.helpful: Find the one thread that runs through all three.
lessons.estrella: Find the thread that repeats, then make it the center.
lessons.fills: Completes the CLEAR band — side-label ignites, name disc brightens to stage one.

lessons.sentence: If you are known for everything, you are known for nothing. Pick one thing people can remember and pass on.
lessons.situation: A friend needs help and is deciding who to call.
choices: The person known for one clear thing.
The person who "does a bit of everything."
Whoever answers the phone first.
lessons.feedback: Exactly. Narrow is easy to remember, so people refer you.
lessons.helpful: The person known for one clear thing.
lessons.estrella: Narrow is not small. Narrow is easy to remember.
lessons.fills: Lights the first PROOF cell (proof-gold-deep).

lessons.idea: Lead with value, not your title.
lessons.sentence: Your bio comes in three sizes, but all of them open with the value you bring, never with your job title.
lessons.situation: You are writing the first line of your bio.
choices: "I am a Senior Manager at..."
"I help family businesses build fair pay people trust."
"Passionate, results-driven professional."
lessons.feedback: Good. The reader leans in instead of waiting for the point.
lessons.helpful: "I help family businesses build fair pay people trust."
lessons.estrella: Open with what you do for people, not your box.
lessons.fills: Lights the second PROOF cell (proof-gold-deep).

lessons.sentence: Your profile is a shop window, not a CV. Under each role, write what you achieved, not what you were assigned.
lessons.situation: You are rewriting a line under your current job.
choices: "Responsible for training programmes."
"Built training that cut new-hire turnover by 30%."
"Handled various HR duties."
lessons.feedback: Yes. A result with a number is what makes a reader trust you.
lessons.helpful: "Built training that cut new-hire turnover by 30%."
lessons.estrella: Swap one duty for one result with a real number.
lessons.fills: Lights the third PROOF cell (proof-gold-deep).

lessons.sentence: Share posts that help the right people. Skip the fake humility and the show-off stories that lose trust.
lessons.situation: You are about to post something on LinkedIn.
choices: "So humbled and blessed to announce my brilliance."
"Three signs your bonus system is losing your best people."
A long story whose only point is how great you are.
lessons.feedback: That works. It helps the reader, so it never feels like bragging.
lessons.helpful: "Three signs your bonus system is losing your best people."
lessons.estrella: Would you say it to a respected colleague over coffee?
lessons.fills: Completes the PROOF band — side-label ignites, name disc brightens to stage two.

lessons.idea: Add one real thing to comments.
lessons.sentence: Posting is half the game. Real relationships grow when every comment adds one new idea, example, or honest question.
lessons.situation: You want to comment on someone's post in your field.
choices: "Great post!"
Add a short example from your own work, then ask a real question.
Drop a link to your own services.
lessons.feedback: Right. A comment that adds value is a tiny post people notice.
lessons.helpful: Add a short example from your own work, then ask a real question.
lessons.estrella: Every comment should add one new idea or question.
lessons.fills: Lights the first USEFUL cell (useful-amber).

lessons.sentence: You do not need to brag. Be useful in public — share, answer, introduce — and being noticed takes care of itself.
lessons.situation: You hate self-promotion but want to be seen.
choices: Announce every win loudly and often.
Answer real questions in public and introduce helpful people.
Wait quietly and hope someone finds your work.
lessons.feedback: Yes. Visibility is just what happens when people see you help.
lessons.helpful: Answer real questions in public and introduce helpful people.
lessons.estrella: Ask how to be useful, not how to be noticed.
lessons.fills: Lights the second USEFUL cell (useful-amber).

lessons.idea: Give your idea a clear name.
lessons.sentence: When you name a useful idea simply, people remember it, repeat it, and credit you. Your name travels on its own.
lessons.situation: You have a handy routine you teach people.
choices: Call it "a thing I do sometimes."
Name it clearly, like "The 15-Minute Round."
Keep it secret so no one copies it.
lessons.feedback: Good. A named idea sticks in memory and gets passed along.
lessons.helpful: Name it clearly, like "The 15-Minute Round."
lessons.estrella: A named idea is easy to repeat and easy to credit.
lessons.fills: Lights the third USEFUL cell (useful-amber).

lessons.idea: Tell people how to use you.
lessons.sentence: A strong brand brings work to you. After you help someone, plant a seed: say plainly what you are open to next.
lessons.situation: You just finished good work for a contact.
choices: Say nothing and hope they remember you.
"If your team ever needs help with pay fairness, I'd be glad to talk."
Send a hard pitch for all your services.
lessons.feedback: That is it. A gentle, clear signal opens the door without pressure.
lessons.helpful: "If your team ever needs help with pay fairness, I'd be glad to talk."
lessons.estrella: People will not guess. Plant the seed so they remember.
lessons.fills: Completes the USEFUL band — final cell lights, name disc beams full gold, halo at full glow.

bigPromise: Hear the real question hiding behind the words, then answer it calmly.

artifact.name: Interview Readiness Ring
artifact.concept: A gold ring that fills as you decode questions and bank ready answers — when it is full, you walk in ready.
artifact.visual: A single 248px gold ring (reusing the V2 ring shell), but the track is split into 12 thin arc-segments grouped into 4 visible quarters (Before the Room, The Answers, Calm Under Pressure, The Offer) by 4 tiny gold tick-marks at 12/3/6/9 o'clock. Empty: each arc is a pale gold-bg hairline (stroke var(--gold-bg)), each quarter labelled by a faint gold dot at its midpoint. Filled: that lesson's arc lights with the gold gradient (var(--gold-soft)->var(--gold)) and a small move-token (a tiny rounded gold chip with the lesson's move icon — ear, magnifier, story, mic, hand) sits docked just inside the arc, like an answer banked into the ring. Center holds a calm emblem (a soft gold spark) over a Cormorant count reading 'N/12 ready' and the label READINESS. As quarters complete, the quarter's faint dot turns solid gold. Full ring (12/12): the whole ring is unbroken gold, the center spark blooms once, and the label reads READY.
artifact.fillUnit: One lesson lights one arc-segment of the ring and docks its move-token inside that arc.
artifact.saveMoment: On Save, the chosen choice's icon lifts off its card as a gold chip (the .fly token) and arcs along a curved path to its slot on the ring; on arrival the target arc-segment sweeps from empty hairline to full gold gradient (stroke-dashoffset animation, ~.6s spring), the chip settles as the docked move-token with a quick scale .3->1.22->1 drop, and the hero fires one soft radial glow pulse (reuse .hero.pulse glow). The count ticks up with a tabular-nums roll. If that arc completes a quarter, the quarter's faint dot fills solid gold with a tiny beat. On the 12th save, the full ring does one slow shimmer sweep and the center spark blooms; everything else stays calm — no confetti.
artifact.renderApproach: Reuse every V2 token verbatim (--bg #FCFAF4, --gold/-soft/-deep, --gold-bg, --sh-gold, --spring, Cormorant for the center count + idea title, Inter for UI). Build the ring as ONE inline SVG: a base <circle> track in var(--gold-bg) (stroke-width 6, the hairline), then 12 <path> arc-segments (or 12 stroke-dasharray slices of a single circle, each its own <circle> with computed stroke-dasharray/offset) so each can independently animate stroke from var(--gold-bg) to a gold linearGradient defID. Add 4 short radial tick <line>s at 0/90/180/270deg for the quarter breaks, and 4 quarter-dots as small <circle>s at the arc midpoints that toggle fill var(--gold-bg)->var(--gold) via a .lit class. Move-tokens are 12 absolutely-positioned 26px divs (rounded 9px, background linear-gradient(145deg,var(--gold-soft),var(--gold)), --sh-gold, inner SVG icon white) placed just inside each arc with the same left/top trig the V2 uses for its 6 nodes (here 12 angles at 30deg steps), hidden by default and revealed with a .drop scale animation on fill. Center: reuse .ring-core / .ring-emblem / .ring-count (swap '/6' for '/12 ready' and label 'READINESS'). The fly animation, glow keyframe, and prefers-reduced-motion guard are copied straight from V2 (reduced motion just toggles classes, no flight). Each lesson screen renders See-It/Pick-It/Get-It below the hero exactly like the V2 lesson block; the ring is the persistent HERO at top.
artifact.accent: gold (#C49A3F / #E6CD86 / #A07A2C) with a single calm green (--ok #4C9C7A) used only for the Estrella 'win' line

lessons.sentence: An interviewer has little time, so most questions quietly check one risk. Answer the worry under the words, not just the words.
lessons.situation: They ask, "Why are you leaving your current job?"
choices: They want your salary history
They are checking if you'll be a problem here too
They are testing labour law
lessons.feedback: Yes. They watch how you speak about old jobs. Stay calm and forward.
lessons.helpful: They are checking if you'll be a problem here too
lessons.estrella: Pause one second. Ask what risk they are really checking.
lessons.fills: Lights the first arc of the ring and docks an 'ear' move-token inside it.

lessons.sentence: Anyone can read the website. The candidate who gets the offer knows the problem the team is worried about right now.
lessons.situation: You have one hour to prepare for a fast-growing company.
choices: Memorise their mission statement
Find a current pain you can help fix
Learn the year they were founded
lessons.feedback: Right. Tie their real pain to one piece of your own proof.
lessons.helpful: Find a current pain you can help fix
lessons.estrella: Facts on the page are the floor. Real problems win.
lessons.fills: Lights the second arc and docks a 'magnifier' move-token.

lessons.sentence: You can't make up good stories under pressure. Keep a small bank of real moments and shape one to fit whatever they ask.
lessons.situation: You spent all week memorising answers word for word.
choices: Memorise more exact answers
Keep a few real stories you can reshape
Plan to make it up on the day
lessons.feedback: Yes. One real story can answer many different questions.
lessons.helpful: Keep a few real stories you can reshape
lessons.estrella: You rarely get the question you practised. Stay flexible.
lessons.fills: Lights the third arc, completing the first quarter; its quarter-dot turns solid gold.

lessons.sentence: A proof story has a quiet shape: short setup, what you did, what happened. Keep the shape hidden so you sound human.
lessons.situation: Your answer comes out as "the situation was... my task was..."
choices: Announce each label out loud
Keep the shape inside, tell it naturally
Skip the result to save time
lessons.feedback: Yes. Spend your time on what you did and what happened.
lessons.helpful: Keep the shape inside, tell it naturally
lessons.estrella: The order lives in your head, not in your words.
lessons.fills: Lights the fourth arc and docks a 'story' move-token.

lessons.sentence: Behavioral questions feel endless but fall into eight groups. One strong story for each, and you are ready for almost any of them.
lessons.situation: You have good wins, but no story about a failure.
choices: Hope the failure question never comes
Find one real failure story now
Make one up in the room
lessons.feedback: Right. Failure questions are common, so fill that gap early.
lessons.helpful: Find one real failure story now
lessons.estrella: The worst time to hunt for a story is live, in the room.
lessons.fills: Lights the fifth arc and docks a 'grid' move-token.

lessons.sentence: The question you dread tests honesty, not perfection. Own the real thing, say what you learned, end on how you grew.
lessons.situation: "Tell me about a time you failed."
choices: Give a fake weakness like "I work too hard"
Own it, then show what you changed
Blame the team around you
lessons.feedback: Yes. End on the growth so the answer rises, not falls.
lessons.helpful: Own it, then show what you changed
lessons.estrella: A hard question, handled calmly, builds more trust than an easy one.
lessons.fills: Lights the sixth arc, completing the second quarter; its dot turns solid gold.

lessons.sentence: Nerves make us rush and fill gaps with "um." A slow pace and a short silent pause make you sound calm and sure.
lessons.situation: You need a moment to think mid-answer.
choices: Fill the gap with "um, you know"
Pause in silence, then speak
Talk faster to escape the gap
lessons.feedback: Yes. A short silent pause reads as a careful, confident mind.
lessons.helpful: Pause in silence, then speak
lessons.estrella: Breathe once before you answer. It resets your pace and nerves.
lessons.fills: Lights the seventh arc and docks a 'mic' move-token.

lessons.sentence: Before you say a word, your body has spoken. Sit upright, open shoulders, feet planted, and you look — and feel — calmer.
lessons.situation: A panel of three is interviewing you.
choices: Stare hard at one person
Answer who asked, then include the others
Keep your eyes on your notes
lessons.feedback: Yes. Share your gaze. It builds trust without staring.
lessons.helpful: Answer who asked, then include the others
lessons.estrella: Sit like you belong there, and you start to feel it.
lessons.fills: Lights the eighth arc and docks a 'posture' move-token.

lessons.sentence: Everyone stumbles. A calm line buys you a moment and stops the panic, so a slip never turns into a spiral.
lessons.situation: Your mind goes blank halfway through an answer.
choices: Apologise many times
Use a calm line, then restate your point
Sit in long silence and hope
lessons.feedback: Yes. A prepared line sounds thoughtful, not lost.
lessons.helpful: Use a calm line, then restate your point
lessons.estrella: A calm recovery can score higher than a perfect answer.
lessons.fills: Lights the ninth arc, completing the third quarter; its dot turns solid gold.

lessons.sentence: An offer is more than the base number. Housing, flights, and school fees can make a lower base the stronger deal.
lessons.situation: Offer A has a higher base. Offer B adds housing and school fees.
choices: Pick the higher base, it's simpler
Add up the full package first
Decide on base salary alone
lessons.feedback: Right. Total the real value before you judge any offer.
lessons.helpful: Add up the full package first
lessons.estrella: Two offers with the same base can be very different.
lessons.fills: Lights the tenth arc and docks a 'package' move-token.

lessons.sentence: Asking for more is not a fight. Open with warmth, give a real reason, name a clear range, and start the job loved.
lessons.situation: You want a higher base than they offered.
choices: Demand more, firmly
Thank them, give a reason, name a range
Threaten to take another offer
lessons.feedback: Yes. Warmth first keeps them open before you give the number.
lessons.helpful: Thank them, give a reason, name a range
lessons.estrella: If the base will not move, trade on flights, bonus, or leave.
lessons.fills: Lights the eleventh arc and docks a 'handshake' move-token.

lessons.sentence: You learn interviews by doing, not reading. Run a mock, push through stumbles, then honestly review the real skills.
lessons.situation: You finished a practice run and felt nervous.
choices: Note only that you felt nervous
Check the real skills, fix one thing
Try to fix everything at once
lessons.feedback: Yes. Fix one or two things per round. That is how progress sticks.
lessons.helpful: Check the real skills, fix one thing
lessons.estrella: The review is where the learning actually happens.
lessons.fills: Lights the twelfth arc, completing the ring; the center spark blooms and the label reads READY.

bigPromise: Know when AI should help with people, and when a person must decide.

artifact.name: The AI Decision Map
artifact.concept: A four-zone map of where AI belongs in people work. Each saved lesson drops one gold dot into the right zone, so the learner literally watches their judgment take shape across the four quadrants.
artifact.visual: A soft rounded square (the map) on warm white, split into four quadrants by faint gold cross-lines. Top-left AUTOMATE (gold-bg tint), top-right HELP, bottom-left KEEP HUMAN, bottom-right NOT READY — each labelled in small uppercase Inter with a tiny line icon (a gear, two hands, a person, a pause bar). The map center holds a small gold compass emblem (a four-point star, same diamond used in V2) over a tabular count like 0/16. Empty state: the four zones sit pale and quiet, only the emblem glowing. As dots land they cluster inside their zone like constellation points, each a 14px gold-gradient dot with a faint ring. Full state: 16 dots scattered across all four zones, the whole map warmly lit, a thin gold border tracing the square. A learner can read at a glance: most dots in HELP and KEEP HUMAN means I learned AI mostly assists, people still decide.
artifact.fillUnit: One gold dot lands inside one of the four zones — the zone that lesson's real teaching points to (Automate, Help, Keep human, or Not ready).
artifact.saveMoment: On Save, the chosen zone card lifts and its icon brightens; a 40px gold-gradient token (the lesson's tiny icon) flies from the picked choice on a 0.6s spring path into that quadrant, scales down to a 14px dot, and lands with a drop bounce (cubic-bezier(.34,1.56,.64,1)). The target zone flashes a soft gold glow (radial blur pulse, like .hero.pulse), the map's center count ticks up by one, and a faint connecting line briefly draws from the new dot to its nearest neighbor in the same zone — the constellation growing. Estrella's note turns warm green.
artifact.renderApproach: Reuse every V2 token verbatim (--bg #FCFAF4, --gold #C49A3F, --gold-soft #E6CD86, --gold-deep #A07A2C, --gold-bg #FBF3DC, --cream-2, shadows --sh-1/2/3/-gold, --spring). HERO = a 248x248 CSS grid of four .zone cards (grid-template:1fr 1fr / 1fr 1fr, gap 2px, gold cross-lines via a pseudo-element). Each .zone is position:relative with a small uppercase label + 18px line-icon top-corner, gold-bg tint that deepens as it gains dots. Dots are absolutely-positioned 14px divs (background:linear-gradient(145deg,var(--gold-soft),var(--gold)); box-shadow:var(--sh-gold)) placed at jittered offsets inside the zone via a small JS layout function (predefined slot coords per zone so they never overlap). Center emblem = the V2 .ring-emblem star + .ring-count (0/16). The fly-token, glow pulse, and lock-after-save logic are the exact V2 JS pattern (getBoundingClientRect from the picked .c-ic to the destination zone, append .fly, force reflow, transition, then remove and render the resting dot). prefers-reduced-motion: skip the flight, just render the dot. Lesson UI below the map is identical to V2: .step eyebrow, Cormorant .idea title, one .say line, one .situation card, .choices (2-3 .choice cards each with c-ic + c-label + c-pick check), one .estrella note, sticky .cta Save button. No third column, no chat, no tables.
artifact.accent: gold (#C49A3F / #E6CD86 / #A07A2C) plus a soft sage-green (#4C9C7A / #ECF6F0) used only for the KEEP HUMAN zone tint and the win-state Estrella note

lessons.sentence: Before you pick a tool, pick the zone. A leave-balance chatbot is safe ground. Guessing who will quit is not.
lessons.situation: A bank wants its first AI win. Someone suggests a bot that answers "how many leave days do I have left?"
choices: Put it in Automate
Put it in Keep human
Put it in Not ready
lessons.feedback: Automate. High volume, clear answers, cheap to fix — safe ground to start.
lessons.helpful: Put it in Automate
lessons.estrella: Tools change. The four jobs of HR stay the same.
lessons.fills: One gold dot lands in the Automate zone.

lessons.idea: AI shifts the work, not the people.
lessons.sentence: When a bot handles lookups, your time moves to the hard cases. Each job keeps one piece that must stay human.
lessons.situation: An AI model shows nurses are leaving fast. It can spot the pattern. Now what?
choices: Let AI choose the fix
Use it, then a person decides
Email the at-risk list to all managers
lessons.feedback: AI finds the pattern; a leader decides the response. That choice stays human.
lessons.helpful: Use it, then a person decides
lessons.estrella: AI drafts. A person decides for anyone you'll meet again.
lessons.fills: One gold dot lands in the Keep human zone.

lessons.idea: Sort by mistake cost and trust.
lessons.sentence: Cheap, easy-to-fix mistakes go to Automate. Life-changing calls stay human. Most work sits in between, where AI helps and a person owns it.
lessons.situation: Finance asks you to let AI decide, on its own, who gets laid off in a downturn.
choices: Automate to save time
Keep human
Let AI help, manager decides
lessons.feedback: Keep human. A mistake here is severe and trust is everything.
lessons.helpful: Keep human
lessons.estrella: Decide your never-list before the pressure arrives.
lessons.fills: One gold dot lands in the Keep human zone.

lessons.sentence: One page: where AI helps most, what result it serves, what stays human, and how you'll know it worked. Then you can say no fast.
lessons.situation: Leadership asks "what's our AI plan?" Two answers are on the table.
choices: "We'll use the most advanced AI everywhere"
"AI for hiring admin, cut shortlist time, no AI in final hiring"
"We'll explore as things come up"
lessons.feedback: The middle one names a focus, a number, and a clear limit. Leaders trust that.
lessons.helpful: "AI for hiring admin, cut shortlist time, no AI in final hiring"
lessons.estrella: A clear plan lets you say no without a long meeting.
lessons.fills: One gold dot lands in the Help zone.

lessons.sentence: Bias slips in through old data, biased labels, and stand-in clues like a postcode or a career gap. Removing names does not remove it.
lessons.situation: A vendor says "our tool can't be biased — it never sees names, gender, or photos."
choices: Accept it — no names, no bias
Ask how it scores by group
Hide their skills too
lessons.feedback: Ask how it scores by group. Stand-in clues carry bias even with names gone.
lessons.helpful: Ask how it scores by group
lessons.estrella: A postcode can quietly stand in for a whole group.
lessons.fills: One gold dot lands in the Not ready zone.

lessons.sentence: Ask five things: the data, the labels, fairness tests, the top features, and the appeal path. A refusal is a red flag, not a discount.
lessons.situation: A cheaper vendor won't share any fairness results — "our model is proprietary."
choices: Buy it — price wins
Mark it red, don't buy until they prove it
Buy it for senior roles only
lessons.feedback: Mark it red. A public bias failure costs far more than the savings.
lessons.helpful: Mark it red, don't buy until they prove it
lessons.estrella: Keep the scored sheet. It's your proof you checked.
lessons.fills: One gold dot lands in the Not ready zone.

lessons.sentence: Ask everyone the same job questions on the same scale. Let AI draft questions and write down answers. Never let it score faces or accents.
lessons.situation: Your panel wants AI in interviews. Three ideas are on the table.
choices: Score facial expressions for confidence
Rank people by accent
Write down answers, flag harsh scoring
lessons.feedback: Write down and flag. It supports fairness without judging the person.
lessons.helpful: Write down answers, flag harsh scoring
lessons.estrella: Humans score the answer. AI never scores the face.
lessons.fills: One gold dot lands in the Help zone.

lessons.sentence: Take the group that passes most. If another group passes below 80% of that rate, stop and look. A healthy total can hide a gap underneath.
lessons.situation: Men pass an AI CV screen at 50%. Women pass at 30%. Eighty percent of 50% is 40%.
choices: Fine — both are above zero
Failed — 30% is below 40%, investigate
Can't tell without more data
lessons.feedback: Failed. 30% sits below the 40% line — a clear flag to investigate.
lessons.helpful: Failed — 30% is below 40%, investigate
lessons.estrella: An overall pass rate can hide a real gap. Split it.
lessons.fills: One gold dot lands in the Not ready zone.

lessons.sentence: What happened, why, what's next, what to do. Most teams can answer the first two. Don't promise a prediction your data can't support.
lessons.situation: Leadership asks "who will resign next quarter?" Your data is messy and you've done no real study yet.
choices: Promise a prediction by next month
Show who left and why first, then build up
Guess some names to look helpful
lessons.feedback: Show who and why first. Naming your rung honestly protects your credibility.
lessons.helpful: Show who left and why first, then build up
lessons.estrella: Often the reason people left is the fix. No forecast needed.
lessons.fills: One gold dot lands in the Help zone.

lessons.sentence: AI can make feedback clearer, but it can also blur hard truths into polite nothing. Name the behaviour and its impact — never just a label.
lessons.situation: A manager types "Ahmed lacks confidence" and asks AI to polish it.
choices: Soften the wording and send
Ask what he did and the impact, then describe that
Add "speak up more" with no proof
lessons.feedback: Ask what he did. Fair feedback ties to a real behaviour, not a personality.
lessons.helpful: Ask what he did and the impact, then describe that
lessons.estrella: Would this help the person improve, or just feel judged?
lessons.fills: One gold dot lands in the Help zone.

lessons.sentence: AI can give each person their next best lesson — but only if you've written down the skills each role needs and what good looks like.
lessons.situation: A vendor offers AI that "personalises learning for everyone." What do you need first?
choices: A bigger budget
A clear list of skills and levels per role
A longer list of courses
lessons.feedback: The skills list. Without it, AI suggests busy work that closes no real gap.
lessons.helpful: A clear list of skills and levels per role
lessons.estrella: Start small: one team, a few skills, then grow.
lessons.fills: One gold dot lands in the Help zone.

lessons.idea: Show the few numbers that move.
lessons.sentence: Keep only numbers that would change a decision. Show the trend, turn it into money or risk, and add one clear next step.
lessons.situation: You want the CEO to act. Three ways to show the same data.
choices: A 20-number dashboard
"Attrition up 4 points" with no context
A trend line, the cost in money, one recommendation
lessons.feedback: Trend, money, recommendation. Leaders act on direction plus a next step.
lessons.helpful: A trend line, the cost in money, one recommendation
lessons.estrella: If a number moving changes nothing, drop it.
lessons.fills: One gold dot lands in the Help zone.

lessons.idea: Ask the people whose data it is.
lessons.sentence: Collect the least you need, for one clear reason, told plainly. And talk to staff or their reps before you build, not after.
lessons.situation: An engagement tool would read private staff messages. The works council asks: does it?
choices: Proceed — you have legal sign-off
Hide the message-reading part
Switch to anonymous, grouped data only
lessons.feedback: Switch to grouped data. You keep the insight without watching individuals.
lessons.helpful: Switch to anonymous, grouped data only
lessons.estrella: At work, a forced tick-box is not real consent.
lessons.fills: One gold dot lands in the Keep human zone.

lessons.sentence: A simple two-column rule: what staff may do with AI, and what they must not. Write it so your newest hire can repeat it back.
lessons.situation: You're sorting lines for the "must not" column of your AI rules.
choices: Draft a job description with approved AI
Paste staff salary and medical data into a public chatbot
Summarise your own meeting notes
lessons.feedback: The salary and medical one. Confidential data in a public tool is a breach.
lessons.helpful: Paste staff salary and medical data into a public chatbot
lessons.estrella: If a junior can explain the rules back, they're clear enough.
lessons.fills: One gold dot lands in the Not ready zone.

lessons.sentence: Ninety days, three steps: set the rules, run one safe pilot, then review and grow. Pick a first win that's safe, useful, and visible.
lessons.situation: You're choosing the first AI project for your 90-day plan.
choices: AI that decides promotions on its own
A leave-and-payroll helpdesk bot, human handover for sensitive cases
AI that scores morale from chat secretly
lessons.feedback: The helpdesk bot. Low stakes, real pain solved, a win people notice.
lessons.helpful: A leave-and-payroll helpdesk bot, human handover for sensitive cases
lessons.estrella: Do rules and consultation first. It feels slow, it saves months.
lessons.fills: One gold dot lands in the Automate zone.

lessons.idea: Keep a real person in charge.
lessons.sentence: A rubber-stamp click is fake safety. Real review needs time, the power to say no, and one named person who owns the call.
lessons.situation: Managers approve every AI budget recommendation in seconds. What does that show?
choices: The AI is perfect, no review needed
Oversight has become a rubber stamp
The managers are very skilled
lessons.feedback: A rubber stamp. Near-zero pushback means people stopped truly checking.
lessons.helpful: Oversight has become a rubber stamp
lessons.estrella: AI advises, humans decide, a named person owns it.
lessons.fills: The final gold dot lands in the Keep human zone — the map is full.

bigPromise: Stop carrying everything alone — protect your energy one move at a time.

artifact.name: The Energy Battery
artifact.concept: A premium gold battery that charges cell by cell as you make one load-protecting choice per lesson — the visual proof that a leader's energy is refillable, not endless.
artifact.visual: A large horizontal battery is the HERO, centered at the top on warm white (#FCFAF4). The battery body is a rounded-rectangle 'shell' (radius ~28px, card white #FFFFFF, 1.5px gold-line stroke, soft layered shadow --sh-3) about 300px wide and 130px tall, with a small gold nub 'tip' on the right end. Inside the shell sit 6 vertical CELLS (rounded bars, ~10px gap) lying side by side — empty cells are pale cream (--gold-bg #FBF3DC) with a faint gold-line outline; a charged cell fills with the gold gradient (linear-gradient(160deg, #E6CD86, #C49A3F)) and gets the gold glow (--sh-gold). Cells fill LEFT to RIGHT so the battery visibly rises. A thin gold charge-level line sweeps up behind the cells as a subtle backing meter. Centered just under the battery: a Cormorant Garamond charge read-out — big gold number e.g. '3' over a muted '/6' — with the tiny uppercase label 'ENERGY CHARGE'. At 0/6 the battery reads empty and slightly dim with the caption 'Make one move to protect your week. Each one charges a cell.' At 6/6 all cells are lit gold, a soft full-battery glow ring breathes once behind the shell, the tip nub turns solid gold, and the caption reads 'Full charge. Your week can hold its load.' Each cell carries a tiny white icon of the move that charged it (keep / hand-off / shield / leaf) so the battery doubles as a memory of the six choices.
artifact.fillUnit: One saved lesson charges exactly one cell — it fills left-to-right with the gold gradient, gains the gold glow, and stamps the chosen move's small white icon inside it. The big charge number ticks up by one.
artifact.saveMoment: On Save: the chosen move card lifts, and its small icon detaches into a floating gold token (.fly, 40px, gold gradient, --sh-gold). It arcs from the choice card to the next empty cell on a 600ms spring (cubic-bezier(.34,1.56,.64,1)). On landing the token dissolves INTO the cell: the cell fills from the bottom up with the gold gradient in one liquid sweep (clip-path or scaleY from 0 to 1, transform-origin bottom), a quick 'charge spark' highlight runs across its top edge, and the whole battery shell pulses once (scale .98 -> 1.0) with a soft gold bloom behind it. The Cormorant charge number flips up with a tiny spring bounce. On the 6th save the empty tip nub snaps to solid gold and a single calm full-charge glow breathes once — no confetti, no noise. Reduced-motion: token and sweep are skipped; the cell simply switches to lit gold and the number updates.
artifact.renderApproach: Pure HTML/CSS/SVG reusing the confidence-simple-v2 tokens verbatim (--bg, --card, --gold, --gold-soft, --gold-deep, --gold-bg, --gold-line, --sh-1/2/3, --sh-gold, --spring, Cormorant Garamond for the charge number + idea title, Inter for UI). BATTERY = one .battery div (the shell) wrapping a flex row of 6 .cell divs plus a .tip nub div. Each .cell is a rounded bar; empty state = background var(--gold-bg) + inset gold-line; charged state added via a .charged class = background:linear-gradient(160deg,var(--gold-soft),var(--gold)); box-shadow:var(--sh-gold); the inner icon (an inline 14px white SVG) fades in. The liquid fill uses a ::before layer inside each cell with transform:scaleY(0);transform-origin:bottom and transitions to scaleY(1) on .charged (so it rises from the base). Charge read-out is a Cormorant number bound to a JS counter (charged cells). The .fly token is created in JS exactly like the V2 mockup (getBoundingClientRect from the chosen .c-ic to the target .cell, appended to body, transitioned over 640ms), then removed and the cell gets .charged. Battery-shell pulse via a one-shot keyframe class. Full-charge glow = a radial-gradient ::after on the shell that animates opacity once when count hits 6. Everything respects @media(prefers-reduced-motion:reduce) by disabling animation and just toggling .charged. No new fonts, no new colors, no dependencies — drops straight into the V2 page skeleton with the ring swapped for the battery.
artifact.accent: gold (#C49A3F / #E6CD86 / #A07A2C) as the charge color, with a single calm sage-green tint (--ok #4C9C7A) reserved only for the Estrella 'win' line and the recover/leaf cell accent — kept inside the Estrella palette

lessons.idea: Burnout warns you before it hits.
lessons.sentence: Burnout leaks in slowly. Three quiet signals show up first: you stay tired after rest, you pull away, you start to doubt the work.
lessons.situation: A full weekend off, and you still feel flat on Monday morning.
choices: Just a tired week
An early warning to act on
Nothing worth noticing
lessons.feedback: Rest that stops refilling you is the real warning. You caught it early.
lessons.helpful: An early warning to act on
lessons.estrella: Tired even after rest? That is a signal, not a mood.
lessons.fills: Charges the first battery cell — the spotting move that starts the whole rhythm.

lessons.sentence: Two hours can cost the same time but very different energy. Some work fuels you, some quietly drains you. Guard the work that fuels you.
lessons.situation: A long status meeting leaves you flat. Coaching your team leaves you sharp.
choices: Keep the long meeting as-is
Shrink the meeting, protect the coaching
Cut everything and hope
lessons.feedback: You moved a drain out and put your best energy where it grows the team.
lessons.helpful: Shrink the meeting, protect the coaching
lessons.estrella: Spend your sharpest hours on the work that fuels you.
lessons.fills: Charges a cell with the 'keep' move — energy you chose to protect.

lessons.sentence: A boundary is a clear rule, not a wall. Say no with warmth in three beats: hear them, decline clearly, offer one small thing you can do.
lessons.situation: Your manager pings a non-urgent task at 9pm. Your rule is offline after 8.
choices: Reply now so you seem keen
Answer in the morning, rule said in advance
Go silent and explain nothing
lessons.feedback: You said no to the task and yes to the relationship. That makes it hold.
lessons.helpful: Answer in the morning, rule said in advance
lessons.estrella: A boundary holds only when you hold it every time.
lessons.fills: Charges a cell with the 'decline' move — a boundary that finally holds.

lessons.idea: Hand it off without dropping it.
lessons.sentence: Match your support to how ready the person is for this exact task. Agree a few checkpoints, then step back and let them own it.
lessons.situation: A strong analyst is leading her first client report for you.
choices: Hand it over and disappear
Set three checkpoints, then give space
Check her work every two hours
lessons.feedback: Checkpoints kept the outcome safe while she truly owned the task.
lessons.helpful: Set three checkpoints, then give space
lessons.estrella: Own the outcome through checkpoints, not by checking every step.
lessons.fills: Charges a cell with the 'hand off' move — weight shared, not dropped.

lessons.sentence: Every switch from real work to a message costs minutes to recover. Block focused time, mute alerts, and promise people a clear return time.
lessons.situation: Your team feels they must answer every chat within minutes, all day.
choices: Stay always available
Block focus time, agree reply speeds
Reply to everything in one minute
lessons.feedback: A clear return time turned focus blocks into trust, not silence.
lessons.helpful: Block focus time, agree reply speeds
lessons.estrella: Promise when you will reply, then protect the quiet in between.
lessons.fills: Charges a cell with the 'focus' move — a block of protected attention.

lessons.sentence: Work like a sprinter: strong effort, then real recovery. Plan rest into your week on purpose so your energy gets refilled before it runs out.
lessons.situation: You run flat-out all week, then crash and start Monday already empty.
choices: Push harder, rest only when wrecked
Plan recovery between strong efforts
Stop all hard work entirely
lessons.feedback: Planned recovery is what makes the next strong push possible. The rhythm holds.
lessons.helpful: Plan recovery between strong efforts
lessons.estrella: Recovery is not lazy — it is what makes the next push possible.
lessons.fills: Charges the final cell with the 'recover' move — battery full, week sustainable.

bigPromise: Turn nerves into one clear move, and get ready to speak.

artifact.name: Talk Stage
artifact.concept: A small warm-lit stage whose footlights turn on and whose curtain rises one step per saved lesson — by lesson 12 the speaker stands fully ready in a fully-lit, fully-open stage. It is a stage, not a ring of dots: a wide shape with a row of lights along the front and a rising meter on the side.
artifact.visual: A rounded stage card on warm white. Across the bottom front edge sits a row of 12 small round footlights. Up the right side runs a slim vertical 'ready' meter with a soft gold-gradient fill. At center stands a simple speaker silhouette on the boards, dim at first. Behind, a soft curtain arch. Empty state: footlights are pale cream, the meter is near-empty, the curtain hangs low and the speaker is faint. Full state: all 12 footlights glow gold, the meter is full to the top with a gentle shimmer, the curtain is fully raised into the arch, and the speaker silhouette is lit warm and standing tall under a soft gold spotlight.
artifact.fillUnit: One saved lesson lights the next footlight along the front edge, nudges the side meter up one of 12 steps, and lifts the curtain a little — and the move you picked (a small icon: open / scissors / pause / question) drops into that footlight as its glow.
artifact.saveMoment: On Save, the chosen move-icon flies from the tapped card down to the next dark footlight. It lands with a spring bounce, the footlight blooms from cream to gold (gold-gradient + sh-gold), a soft glow pulse ripples out from it across the stage floor, the side meter springs up one step, the curtain eases up a notch, and the center speaker brightens slightly under the spotlight. A tiny shimmer crosses the meter. Estrella's line turns calm-green for the win.
artifact.renderApproach: Single stage card (border-radius 24px, --card on --bg radial wash, --sh-3). Build the stage as an inline SVG viewBox 0 0 360 230. Curtain arch = two side drapes filled with a subtle --gold-bg→--cream gradient that translate upward (translateY) per step via a CSS var --raise. Speaker = simple silhouette at center, opacity/filter brightening with --lit. Footlights = a row of 12 r=7 along y≈210, default fill --gold-bg stroke --gold-line; lit ones get fill:url(#goldGrad) + filter drop-shadow(--sh-gold) and a one-shot 'bloom' keyframe (scale .3→1.22→1, the V2 'drop' easing). Side meter = a rounded-rect track (fill --gold-bg) with an inner rect whose height = step/12, filled linear-gradient(--gold-soft→--gold), clipped to rounded corners, animated height with --spring; a thin highlight rect sweeps once on fill (shimmer keyframe). Center readout uses Cormorant Garamond: big tabular count n/12 + label 'TALK STAGE' in --mute 11px .14em uppercase. Glow pulse = an absolutely-positioned radial-gradient div over the stage floor running the V2 @keyframes glow on save. Fly token reuses the V2 .fly element: clone the picked choice's icon, animate left/top from card to the target footlight center over .6s --spring, then remove and trigger bloom. All colors via the V2 tokens; honor prefers-reduced-motion by skipping fly/glow and setting end states directly.
artifact.accent: gold (#C49A3F / #E6CD86 / #A07A2C) with a warm stage-light amber wash (--gold-bg #FBF3DC) as the only secondary tint; win state borrows the V2 calm green --ok #4C9C7A for Estrella only

lessons.idea: Stage fright is your body, not you
lessons.sentence: A racing heart before you speak is just energy with nowhere to go. It is normal, and you can use it.
lessons.situation: Your heart pounds right before you stand up to present.
choices: My body is wrong with me
This is energy I can use
I should cancel the talk
lessons.feedback: Yes. That fast heart is fuel, not a warning.
lessons.helpful: This is energy I can use
lessons.estrella: Read the racing heart as ready, not danger.
lessons.fills: Footlight 1 lights gold and the curtain lifts its first notch.

lessons.sentence: Freezing happens most at the start. Knowing your exact first sentence removes the riskiest moment so you begin sure.
lessons.situation: You walk to the front and your mind goes blank.
choices: Wing the opening
Say a planned first line
Apologise for being nervous
lessons.feedback: Good. A known first line carries you in.
lessons.helpful: Say a planned first line
lessons.estrella: Decide your first sentence before you stand up.
lessons.fills: Footlight 2 lights and the side meter steps up.

lessons.sentence: Fear and excitement feel the same in your body. Saying "I am excited" with a real reason points that energy forward.
lessons.situation: You feel shaky and tense backstage before you go on.
choices: Tell yourself to calm down
Say "I am excited" with a reason
Stand still and wait
lessons.feedback: Nice. Same energy, better direction.
lessons.helpful: Say "I am excited" with a reason
lessons.estrella: Shifting to excited is easier than forcing calm.
lessons.fills: Footlight 3 lights and the speaker brightens a little.

lessons.sentence: Say too much and people remember none of it. Pick one line you want them to repeat, with three points under it.
lessons.situation: You have eight big points for a short talk.
choices: Keep all eight points
Cut to one message, three points
Add a few more to be safe
lessons.feedback: Right. One clear line beats ten blurry ones.
lessons.helpful: Cut to one message, three points
lessons.estrella: If you can't say it in one line, keep cutting.
lessons.fills: Footlight 4 lights and the curtain rises higher.

lessons.sentence: People forget facts but remember stories. A short story works only when its lesson lands right on your message.
lessons.situation: You have a fun story but it doesn't fit your point.
choices: Tell it anyway, it's funny
Cut it or link it to the point
Add more detail to the story
lessons.feedback: Yes. A story must serve the message.
lessons.helpful: Cut it or link it to the point
lessons.estrella: Keep the story only if its lesson hits your point.
lessons.fills: Footlight 5 lights and the meter climbs.

lessons.sentence: Never open with an apology. Hook them in thirty seconds, then close by returning to that idea with one clear ask.
lessons.situation: You start with "Sorry, I didn't have much time."
choices: Open with a sorry
Open with a hook, close with an ask
Open with a long intro
lessons.feedback: Good. The hook earns the next two minutes.
lessons.helpful: Open with a hook, close with an ask
lessons.estrella: No apologies. Start with a hook, end with an ask.
lessons.fills: Footlight 6 lights and the spotlight grows warmer.

lessons.sentence: Nerves make you rush. Speaking a little slower and pausing after a key point makes you sound calm and clear.
lessons.situation: People say your talks are hard to follow.
choices: Speak faster to fit it in
Slow down and add pauses
Use bigger words
lessons.feedback: Yes. Silence beats "um" every time.
lessons.helpful: Slow down and add pauses
lessons.estrella: What feels too slow to you sounds clear to them.
lessons.fills: Footlight 7 lights and the meter steps up.

lessons.sentence: Rocking and hidden hands leak nerves. A grounded stance and open hands make you look calm and trustworthy.
lessons.situation: You keep shifting your weight foot to foot.
choices: Keep moving to seem natural
Stand grounded, hands open
Grip the lectern tight
lessons.feedback: Good. Still by default, move on purpose.
lessons.helpful: Stand grounded, hands open
lessons.estrella: Be still by default; move only on purpose.
lessons.fills: Footlight 8 lights and the speaker stands taller.

lessons.sentence: A slide full of text makes you a slide-reader. One short headline and one image keeps eyes on you, not the screen.
lessons.situation: Your slide has full sentences you read aloud.
choices: Read the full slide aloud
One headline, one visual
Add more bullet points
lessons.feedback: Right. The slide supports you, not replaces you.
lessons.helpful: One headline, one visual
lessons.estrella: If the slide works without you, it's a document.
lessons.fills: Footlight 9 lights and the curtain nears the top.

lessons.sentence: A sharp question raises your heart rate. Pause, breathe, rephrase it, then answer the real concern calmly.
lessons.situation: Someone asks a sharp, challenging question.
choices: Answer fast and firm
Pause, breathe, then answer
Match their tone
lessons.feedback: Yes. Your calm is your power here.
lessons.helpful: Pause, breathe, then answer
lessons.estrella: Never match their heat. Pause, then answer calmly.
lessons.fills: Footlight 10 lights and the meter rises.

lessons.sentence: Each room has its own rules. With a busy board, lead with your answer and ask first, then give detail only if wanted.
lessons.situation: You must give a recommendation to a busy board.
choices: Build up, reveal it last
Lead with the answer and ask
Tell a long story first
lessons.feedback: Good. Busy rooms want the point up front.
lessons.helpful: Lead with the answer and ask
lessons.estrella: Read the room first; then pick how you say it.
lessons.fills: Footlight 11 lights and the spotlight widens.

lessons.sentence: Reading in your head is not rehearsal. Saying your talk out loud, standing, three times is what makes you ready.
lessons.situation: You plan to just read your slides once tonight.
choices: Read it silently once
Say it out loud, standing, three times
Just think it through
lessons.feedback: Yes. The third run is where it flows.
lessons.helpful: Say it out loud, standing, three times
lessons.estrella: Out loud, standing, three times. That is rehearsal.
lessons.fills: Footlight 12 lights, the curtain opens full, the speaker glows ready.

bigPromise: See one people problem, then pick the right next move for it.

artifact.name: HR Action Map
artifact.concept: A small four-district city map of your HR work; each lesson drops one glowing action pin into the district it belongs to, and the map slowly lights up as a whole.
artifact.visual: A soft warm-white rounded panel (radius 28px, --card, --sh-3) holding a gentle four-quadrant map split by faint gold seams (--gold-line). Four labelled districts, each its own quiet zone tint over cream: PEOPLE (top-left, --cream-2), BUSINESS (top-right, --gold-bg), RISK (bottom-left, soft sage wash using --ok at 8%), MANAGER (bottom-right, --cream). A small gold compass emblem sits at the exact center where the seams cross (46px circle, radial gold-soft to gold-deep, --sh-gold), with a tiny tabular count beneath it reading n/12. Each district holds 3 faint empty pin-sockets (12px dashed gold-line circles) — 12 sockets total, 3 per zone. Empty state: districts are pale and washed-out, sockets are hollow dashed outlines, the center compass glows but the map around it is dim and sleepy. Full state: all 12 sockets hold lit gold map-pins (teardrop pins, gradient gold-soft to gold, white glyph inside, --sh-gold), each district has warmed to full saturation, and faint gold route-lines have drawn themselves from the center compass out to every lit pin so the whole map reads as one connected city of decisions. A district visibly 'wakes up' (tint deepens, label brightens from --mute to --gold-deep) the moment its first pin lands.
artifact.fillUnit: One lit gold map-pin dropped into one district socket (the district that matches the lesson's real move: People, Business, Risk, or Manager), plus a short gold route-line drawn from the center compass to that new pin.
artifact.saveMoment: On Save, the chosen answer's small glyph lifts off its choice card as a gold .fly token (the existing V2 fly element), arcs across to the correct district's next empty socket, and lands as a teardrop pin with a .drop spring (scale .3 to 1.22 to 1). At impact: the pin lights (gradient gold-soft to gold), a soft gold route-line strokes itself outward from the center compass to the pin over ~500ms, the whole district tint deepens one step, the center count ticks up by one with a quick number flip, and the hero fires the V2 .pulse glow halo once. If this pin is the district's first, the district label springs from grey to gold-deep and a tiny 'District live' shimmer passes over that quadrant.
artifact.renderApproach: Reuse every V2 token verbatim (--bg #FCFAF4, --card, --cream/--cream-2, gold ramp --gold/--gold-soft/--gold-deep/--gold-bg, --gold-line, --ok, the --sh-1/2/3/gold shadows, --spring cubic-bezier(.34,1.56,.64,1), Cormorant Garamond for the big idea title + center count, Inter for all UI). Build the hero as a single .map panel (CSS Grid, grid-template:1fr 1fr / 1fr 1fr, ~3px gold-seam gaps via background gold-line showing through gap). Each of the 4 cells is a .district with position:relative, a 1-line uppercase label (11px, letter-spacing .14em, color --mute, transitions to --gold-deep on wake) and an absolutely/flex-positioned row of 3 .socket nodes (12px dashed gold-line circles). The center compass is one absolutely-positioned 46px .compass element pinned at 50%/50% over the seam crossing, z-index above seams, holding the Cormorant n/12 count just below it. Pins are .pin elements swapped into sockets on save: teardrop via border-radius:50% 50% 50% 0 + rotate(45deg) wrapper with an upright glyph inside, fill linear-gradient(145deg,gold-soft,gold), box-shadow --sh-gold. Route-lines are thin SVG <line>/<path> strokes (stroke:gold-soft, stroke-width:2, opacity ~.5) inside one full-bleed <svg> layer behind the pins; animate with stroke-dasharray/stroke-dashoffset draw-on over .5s --spring. District wake = toggling a .live class that deepens the cell background tint and brightens the label. Save animation reuses the V2 .fly token + getBoundingClientRect from-choice-glyph to-target-socket, then on arrival add .lit/.drop to the pin and draw its route-line. Honor prefers-reduced-motion exactly as V2 does (skip fly + route draw, just place the lit pin). One screen, hero on top, lesson card below, single dark Save button that becomes the gold .go button after save.
artifact.accent: sage green (--ok #4C9C7A) used only as the quiet wash + pin tint for the RISK district, so risk reads as a distinct calm zone against the three gold-warm districts; everything else stays gold.

lessons.idea: HR does four jobs, not one
lessons.sentence: HR is more than payroll and paperwork. It also helps the business plan, guides change, and speaks up for people.
lessons.situation: Your HR team is fast and tidy, but never gets asked to join the big planning meetings.
choices: You are great at the daily admin
You are missing the planning side
Both are true right now
lessons.feedback: Right. Strong admin is the base, but planning is the job they are missing.
lessons.helpful: You are missing the planning side
lessons.estrella: Strong admin earns trust. Planning earns a seat at the table.
lessons.fills: Drops the first pin into the MANAGER district and wakes it up.

lessons.sentence: Leaders listen when you show the cost. Say the people problem, what it costs the business, then your simple fix.
lessons.situation: A manager says sales are dropping and asks you to book a training day.
choices: Book the training day fast
First find out why sales dropped
Tell him it is not your job
lessons.feedback: Yes. Find the real cause first, or the training may fix nothing.
lessons.helpful: First find out why sales dropped
lessons.estrella: Start with the business problem, not the quick activity.
lessons.fills: Drops a pin into the BUSINESS district and wakes it up.

lessons.idea: Link people plans to the goal
lessons.sentence: Draw a line from the company goal, to the skill it needs, to your HR action. No line means it may not be worth doing.
lessons.situation: The company wants to open three new stores, but HR is planning a values poster campaign.
choices: Run the posters, culture helps
Check if it builds what the goal needs
Cancel everything that is not hiring
lessons.feedback: Right. If the action does not build what the goal needs, move it down.
lessons.helpful: Check if it builds what the goal needs
lessons.estrella: If you cannot draw the line to the goal, question it.
lessons.fills: Drops a pin into the BUSINESS district.

lessons.idea: Start new hires the right way
lessons.sentence: A great hire who quits in three months is a loss. Ask everyone the same questions, then give a clear, welcoming first month.
lessons.situation: A new hire has a laptop and a desk, but no first task and nobody to ask.
choices: Give a longer probation
Give a clear goal, a buddy, a plan
Add more forms to sign
lessons.feedback: Yes. A clear goal and a buddy keep new people from leaving early.
lessons.helpful: Give a clear goal, a buddy, a plan
lessons.estrella: New hires decide early. Make week one feel clear and welcome.
lessons.fills: Drops a pin into the PEOPLE district and wakes it up.

lessons.sentence: Most growth comes from real work and coaching, not just courses. Ask your best people what they want next, before they leave.
lessons.situation: Your strongest engineer is quiet and doing well, so his manager leaves him alone.
choices: Leave him alone, he is fine
Ask now what he wants to learn next
Wait for the exit interview
lessons.feedback: Right. Ask while you can still act, not after he has quit.
lessons.helpful: Ask now what he wants to learn next
lessons.estrella: The stay talk comes before the exit talk. Ask early.
lessons.fills: Drops a pin into the PEOPLE district.

lessons.sentence: Everyone who stays watches how you treat people who leave. Be clear, kind, and brief, and follow the law on pay and notice.
lessons.situation: You must let someone go because their role is no longer needed.
choices: Send the news by email
Be clear, kind, brief, follow the law
Avoid the real reason
lessons.feedback: Yes. Honest and kind, with correct pay and notice, protects everyone.
lessons.helpful: Be clear, kind, brief, follow the law
lessons.estrella: How you say goodbye tells the whole team what you value.
lessons.fills: Drops a pin into the RISK district and wakes it up.

lessons.idea: Feedback often beats once a year
lessons.sentence: The yearly review comes too late to help. Set one clear goal and have short, regular check-ins so problems get caught early.
lessons.situation: A manager saves all her feedback for one long review in December.
choices: Keep the yearly review only
Add short monthly check-ins
Stop giving feedback at all
lessons.feedback: Right. Small regular chats catch problems while there is still time.
lessons.helpful: Add short monthly check-ins
lessons.estrella: Catch the slip in month two, not in month twelve.
lessons.fills: Drops a pin into the MANAGER district.

lessons.idea: Pay is not the only reward
lessons.sentence: You cannot always win on salary. Growth, flexibility, respect, and recognition keep people, and often cost far less.
lessons.situation: A small firm keeps losing people to bigger companies that pay more.
choices: Borrow money to match every offer
Win on growth, flexibility, recognition
Tell people pay does not matter
lessons.feedback: Yes. Small firms win on the rewards big firms forget to give.
lessons.helpful: Win on growth, flexibility, recognition
lessons.estrella: Once pay is fair, more money rarely keeps people longer.
lessons.fills: Drops a pin into the PEOPLE district.

lessons.sentence: Avoiding a hard talk lets it grow. Name the moment, the exact action, and its effect, and stay calm if they react.
lessons.situation: A team leader's reports are often late and you need to tell him.
choices: Say he is lazy and careless
Name the moment, the action, the effect
Wait and hope it fixes itself
lessons.feedback: Right. Talk about the action, not the person, so he can fix it.
lessons.helpful: Name the moment, the action, the effect
lessons.estrella: Stick to facts and actions. Never attack the person.
lessons.fills: Drops a pin into the MANAGER district.

lessons.sentence: Culture is how things really work here, not the poster on the wall. Who you promote teaches people what truly matters.
lessons.situation: A company says it values teamwork but only ever promotes loud solo stars.
choices: Print bigger values posters
Promote people who lift the team
Send a company-wide email
lessons.feedback: Yes. People learn from who gets rewarded, not from slogans.
lessons.helpful: Promote people who lift the team
lessons.estrella: Culture is the worst behaviour leaders quietly allow.
lessons.fills: Drops a pin into the PEOPLE district.

lessons.sentence: Good intentions do not protect you in a dispute. Clear records of warnings, decisions, and reasons do.
lessons.situation: A manager warned someone fairly several times but kept nothing in writing.
choices: No risk, he acted fairly
Without records, fairness is hard to prove
He can recall it all from memory
lessons.feedback: Right. If it is not written down, it may not count later.
lessons.helpful: Without records, fairness is hard to prove
lessons.estrella: Document as you go, not after the problem appears.
lessons.fills: Drops a pin into the RISK district.

lessons.sentence: Counting courses run looks busy but proves nothing. Track numbers a leader would actually act on, like losing your best people.
lessons.situation: Your report proudly shows how many training sessions HR delivered this year.
choices: Number of sessions delivered
Turnover of your best people
Number of surveys sent out
lessons.feedback: Yes. A good number changes a decision; a vanity number just looks busy.
lessons.helpful: Turnover of your best people
lessons.estrella: Ask: if this number moved, would a leader actually act?
lessons.fills: Drops the final pin into the RISK district and completes the map.

bigPromise: Know your number and ask for it without fear.

artifact.name: The Offer Stack
artifact.concept: A tower you build offer by offer — six gold bars stack up while a confidence meter beside it climbs from Quiet to Sure. It is the visual proof that you are now ready to ask.
artifact.visual: Center-left: a vertical STACK of six horizontal bars resting on a thin gold base plate, growing UPWARD from the bottom. Empty bars are slim ghost outlines in --gold-line on warm white; a filled bar is a chunky rounded glass slab with a soft gold-gradient fill (--gold-soft to --gold), a tiny inset icon on its left and a one-word label etched on its right. Each new bar sits ON TOP of the last, so the tower visibly gets taller. Right edge: a slim vertical CONFIDENCE METER — a rounded capsule track in --gold-bg with a gold-gradient fill that rises as bars are added, and three tiny tick labels up its side: Quiet (bottom), Ready (middle), Sure (top). A small Cormorant numeral floats at the top of the stack showing height (0/6 → 6/6). Empty state: one faint ghost base plate, meter sitting at the floor, caption 'Build your offer. Each move stacks up.' Full state: six glowing slabs, meter pinned at Sure, a soft gold halo behind the whole tower and the cap line reads 'Stack complete. You know your number — go ask.'
artifact.fillUnit: One saved lesson lays exactly ONE new gold bar onto the top of the stack (with its icon + one-word label) and lifts the confidence meter by one sixth toward Sure.
artifact.saveMoment: On Save: the chosen card's small icon detaches and FLIES as a gold token (the .fly element, gradient --gold-soft→--gold, soft --sh-gold) on a 0.6s spring arc to the next empty slot at the top of the stack. On arrival the ghost bar SLAMS into a solid slab — a quick scale .3→1.22→1 'drop' bounce — its gold fill sweeps left-to-right, and a soft radial gold glow (the hero ::before) pulses outward 1.1s. In the same beat the confidence meter fill animates UP one step with the spring easing and the height numeral ticks up. A faint chime of light: 3 tiny gold sparkles rise off the new bar and fade. Reduced-motion: token skips the arc, bar fills in place, meter steps up instantly.
artifact.renderApproach: Pure HTML/CSS/SVG reusing every V2 token verbatim (--bg #FCFAF4, --gold #C49A3F / --gold-soft #E6CD86 / --gold-deep #A07A2C, --gold-bg, --sh-1/2/3, --sh-gold, --spring cubic-bezier(.34,1.56,.64,1), Cormorant Garamond for the height numeral + idea title, Inter for UI). STRUCTURE: a flex hero like V2 but the artifact is a horizontal flex row: .stack (the bars) on the left, .meter (capsule) on the right, both inside a centered .offer-stack block max-width ~300px. THE STACK: a flex-column-REVERSE of 6 .bar divs anchored to a 3px --gold base plate, so index 0 sits at the bottom; gap ~10px. Empty .bar = height 34px, border 1.5px dashed-feel solid --gold-line, border-radius 12px, transparent fill. Filled .bar.lit = background linear-gradient(135deg,var(--gold-soft),var(--gold)), border-color --gold, box-shadow var(--sh-gold), with a left .bar-ic (18px inline SVG, white) and right .bar-tag (11px/700 uppercase letter-spacing .12em, color #3a2c08). Stack height label = Cormorant 30px 'N/6' floating above the top bar. THE METER: a 12px-wide rounded capsule, height = stack height, track --gold-bg inset shadow; .meter-fill is an absolutely-positioned bottom-anchored div, height = (N/6*100)%, background linear-gradient(0deg,var(--gold-deep),var(--gold-soft)), border-radius 99px, transition height .55s var(--spring); 3 tiny ::tick labels (Quiet/Ready/Sure) in --mute 10px up the right side. ANIMATION HOOKS reuse V2 names exactly: .drop keyframes for the slam, .glow for the halo (hero.pulse::before), .fly for the token flight, copy the lightRing() pattern but call it stackBar() — set bar.innerHTML to the flown icon, add .lit .drop, bump --meter fill height var and the Cormorant count. The whole tower scales down ~0.9 on mobile and keeps the same vertical anatomy. No dots, no ring — a tower + gauge, unmistakably its own toy.
artifact.accent: gold (#C49A3F / #E6CD86 / #A07A2C) with one calm slate-blue tint (#5B6B82) used only on the empty meter ticks and the 'Quiet' baseline label, so the climb toward warm gold reads as growing confidence

lessons.idea: Spend most of your story on action.
lessons.sentence: When they ask about a time you did something, give a quick setup, then most of your words on what you actually did, and end with a result.
lessons.situation: They ask: tell me about a time you handled a tight deadline.
choices: Two minutes of background, then 'it worked out'
Quick setup, then mostly what I did, end with a number
Jump straight to the happy ending
lessons.feedback: Yes. They want to see you act, so let your actions carry the story.
lessons.helpful: Quick setup, then mostly what I did, end with a number
lessons.estrella: The middle is the story. Make your actions the longest part.
lessons.fills: First gold bar lays down. Meter lifts off the floor toward Ready. Tag: STORY.

lessons.sentence: Lead with your main point in the first sentence, add one or two reasons, then stop, because long answers leak nerves and lose the room.
lessons.situation: They ask: why should we hire you?
choices: Talk fast and keep adding sentences to feel safe
Main point first, one or two reasons, then stop
Give every detail so they know I'm thorough
lessons.feedback: Right. A short, steady answer sounds more sure than a long one.
lessons.helpful: Main point first, one or two reasons, then stop
lessons.estrella: Knowing when to stop is its own kind of confidence.
lessons.fills: Second gold bar stacks on top. Meter climbs a step. Tag: CALM.

lessons.idea: End with a question about their problem.
lessons.sentence: When they ask if you have questions, ask about the role, the team, or their biggest challenge, not about pay or holidays yet.
lessons.situation: The interview ends. They ask: any questions for us?
choices: How many holiday days do I get?
What does success look like in the first six months?
What's the salary range?
lessons.feedback: Good. That question shows you're already thinking like part of the team.
lessons.helpful: What does success look like in the first six months?
lessons.estrella: Save pay questions for after the offer. Ask about their world first.
lessons.fills: Third gold bar locks in. Meter passes the Ready mark. Tag: READ.

lessons.idea: Know your floor, target, and reach.
lessons.sentence: Before any pay talk, set three numbers from real research: your floor you'd still accept, your target you really want, and a reach a little above.
lessons.situation: Early on they ask: what salary are you expecting?
choices: Give my lowest number so I seem easy
Pass it back: what range did you have in mind?
Say I'm flexible and happy with anything
lessons.feedback: Yes. Let them name a number first, so it doesn't pull you down.
lessons.helpful: Pass it back: what range did you have in mind?
lessons.estrella: The first number named pulls the talk. Let it be theirs.
lessons.fills: Fourth gold bar stacks up. Meter nears Sure. Tag: NUMBER.

lessons.idea: Ask for more with warmth, then go quiet.
lessons.sentence: If the offer is below your target, thank them warmly, make one clear ask with a reason, then stay silent and let them answer.
lessons.situation: The offer lands a bit below your target.
choices: Say yes fast so you don't seem difficult
Thank them, ask for your number with a reason, then wait
Tell them the number feels insulting
lessons.feedback: That's it. The quiet after your ask does the work, so don't fill it.
lessons.helpful: Thank them, ask for your number with a reason, then wait
lessons.estrella: Warmth keeps it friendly. Silence after the ask keeps it strong.
lessons.fills: Fifth gold bar settles in. Meter reaches the Sure band. Tag: ASK.

lessons.idea: Practice out loud, fix one thing.
lessons.sentence: Say your answer out loud, then pick the single biggest weakness and redo it, because the skill lives in your mouth, not your head.
lessons.situation: Your practice answer had lots of small flaws.
choices: Try to fix all of them in the next answer
Pick the one biggest weakness and redo it
Skip the feedback and just answer more questions
lessons.feedback: Yes. One fix at a time is how the answer really gets stronger.
lessons.helpful: Pick the one biggest weakness and redo it
lessons.estrella: One clear fix beats ten you can't hold at once.
lessons.fills: Sixth gold bar caps the tower. Meter pins at Sure and the whole stack glows. Tag: READY.
